title: Chapter 24: The New Order
content: The Primordial Council sued for peace.

Not because they wanted to. Because they had no choice. Roxanne had bought their entire enforcement apparatus, forgiven the debts of every human in their territory, and turned the Nether-Grid Exchange into a neutral zone. The old system was collapsing, and they could not stop it.
War, Conquest, and Death appeared at the campus gate for the second and final time.
They looked smaller than before. Not physically — they were still towering cosmic entities — but their presence had diminished. The weight they carried was lighter.
"We accept your terms," Death said. "The Nether-Grid will be restructured. Souls will no longer be used as currency. Existing debt contracts will be phased out over a transition period."
"How long?"
"Five years."
"Two."
"Three."
"Done."
War's molten form flickered. "You negotiate like a Primordial."
"I learned from the best." She glanced at Silas. "And I had a good teacher."
"The Void remains sealed," Conquest said. "This is not negotiable."
"I don't want to open it. I don't know what's on the other side, and I'm not curious enough to find out."
All three Primordials seemed to... relax. As much as cosmic entities could relax.
"Then we have an agreement."
"We have an agreement."
Death stepped forward. Held out her hand. Roxanne shook it.
The moment their hands touched, a pulse rippled through the Nether-Grid. Every contract. Every ledger. Every debt record. The terms of the new agreement were written into the fabric of the system.

The apocalypse officially ended.
\n---\n
All three Primordials seemed to... relax. As much as cosmic entities could relax.
"Then we have an agreement."
"We have an agreement."
Death stepped forward. Held out her hand. Roxanne shook it.
The moment their hands touched, a pulse rippled through the Nether-Grid. Every contract in the Exchange flickered. Every ledger updated simultaneously. Every debt record was flagged for review.
The terms of the New Accord were written into the fabric of the system itself.
War's molten form dimmed further. "The transition will be difficult. There are entities in the deep Nether-Grid who have built their entire existence on the old system. They will resist."
"Let them resist. I have the card. I have the Enforcement Division. And I have a god who makes excellent coffee."
"The coffee is irrelevant," War said flatly.
"The coffee is essential. We're building a new civilization. Civilizations run on caffeine."
Conquest's hundred eyes blinked in a wave.
"You're impossible."
"I'm an art student with infinite money. 'Impossible' is my baseline."
Death smiled. A thin, genuine expression. "The Void chose its heir well."
"The Void didn't choose me. I chose myself."
"Same thing."
The three Primordials departed as they had arrived — through a tear in the violet sky. But this time, the tear sealed cleanly behind them, leaving no scar.
Lena appeared beside Roxanne, charcoal-stained fingers clutching her sketchbook.
"Did that just happen?"
"What?"
"You just negotiated a peace treaty with the three most powerful beings in existence. And you made them agree to abolish soul-debt."
"I did."
"In the middle of the campus quad."
"It's my quad. I bought it."
Lena stared at her. Then she started laughing. Hysterical, relieved laughter that bent her over at the waist.
"You're insane."
"Thank you."
"It wasn't a compliment."
"I'm taking it as one."
That evening, Roxanne sat on the roof of the Fine Arts building, watching the violet sky darken to the deep indigo of Nether-Grid night.
Silas sat beside her. His shadow-coat had settled into something more casual — still dark, but less formal. He was learning to relax.
"The Council will try to undermine the Accord," he said. "They won't attack openly — the barrier and the Enforcement units prevent that. But they'll find ways to work around it."
"Let them. Every time they try, I'll buy whatever they're using and forgive whoever they're exploiting."
"You can't buy everything."
"I can buy a lot of things."
"True."
They sat in comfortable silence for a while.
"What happens when you've fixed everything?" he asked.
"Fixed everything?"
"The Nether-Grid. The debt system. The apocalypse. When there's nothing left to buy, what do you do?"
She considered this.
"I finish my thesis."
"Your thesis."
"The deconstructed Botticelli Venus with the black card. It feels more relevant now than when I started it."
He looked at her with those ancient red eyes, and something in his expression softened.
"You're genuinely going to go back to art school after all of this."
"I'm going to try. The apocalypse interrupted my education. I'm not letting it steal my degree."
He laughed. A quiet, genuine sound.
"You are, without question, the most stubborn human being I have ever encountered."
"Thank you."
"It wasn't a compliment."
"I'm taking it as one."
The Accord was signed at dawn.

Roxanne stood in the main quad, the three Primordials before her. War's molten form had cooled to a deep amber. Conquest's hundred eyes were half-closed — not in exhaustion, but in acceptance. Death looked almost peaceful.

"The terms are recorded," Death said. "The Nether-Grid will begin restructuring within the month. The transition will be managed by a joint committee — one representative from each Primordial lineage, plus one from your territory."
"I want Lena on that committee."
"The artist?"
"She's the most honest person I know. She'll keep everyone honest."
"Agreed."

War's form flickered. "Your card remains active. The Primordial Council will not challenge your ownership. But I warn you: the Exchange does not forget. There are powers in the Nether-Grid older than the Council, older than the Charter. They have been watching."
"Let them watch."
"They will do more than watch eventually."
"Then I'll deal with them when they do."
War almost smiled. Almost.
"You are the most dangerous human being I have ever encountered."
"I'm not dangerous. I'm solvent."
